faqs.q: What does PRN mean on a prescription?
faqs.a: PRN means a medication is meant to be taken only when it's needed, in response to a symptom, rather than at the same time every day. The term comes from a Latin phrase meaning as the situation requires, and in everyday practice people just say as needed.

faqs.q: Does as needed mean I can take it whenever I want?
faqs.a: No. A PRN label still spells out limits, including the most you can take in a day and the minimum time to wait between doses. As needed has a ceiling, and going past it can be dangerous, so check the label and ask your prescriber if it's unclear.

faqs.q: Are PRN medications safe or non-addictive?
faqs.a: PRN status doesn't mean a medication is harmless. Some PRN medications, especially benzodiazepines, carry a risk of dependence with frequent use, which is exactly why clinicians set firm limits. If you find yourself reaching for a PRN medication more and more often, that's worth raising with the prescriber.
